The Belfast braves are badly off for arguments.
Some days ago we received a slip stamped “ W. & G.
Baird.”” This represents an anti-Irish firm that run
an Orange sort of an cvening paper in Belfast. It
appears that the Dublin Typographical Provident
Society recently adopted a resolution demanding that
all she initial printing work in connection with the
forthcoming Irish Parliament should be executed in
the City of Dublin and by trade unionists. With
characteristic muddleheadedness W. & G. Baird
attempt to make a grievance out of this, and so im-
portant do they consider their grievance that they
actually send us by.post a slip containing the great
item from their paper. But surely W. & G. Baird
would not so far backslide from General Carson as to
do printing for the Irish Parliament! Would not that
be recognising the Irish Parliament? And supposing
the Irish Parlinment disputed the bill, “ W. & G.
Baird '’ as loyal Carsonites could only have recourse
to the Courts of the Provisional Government if the:
desired legal redress! And the Irish Parliament will
not recognise Carson’s writ. *“ W. & G. Baird "’ mus$
have more time than brains just now when they go to
the trouble to post us a stamped slip containing the
silly comment in their evening paper on the resolution
of the Dublin Typographical Provident Society. Even
before Home Rule is established it is significant to see
that ““ W. & G. Baird "’ are sniffing after the contracts.
Wooden rifles and rhetorical ‘‘last ditches’” are all
very well, but the salient fact is that ‘* W. & G.. Baird
are in the printing business. :

The Protestant Bishop of Tuam has been -almost
patronising the matives of this country. He oalls the
mer_nbers of his sect ‘“‘Irish Churchmen,’’ whilst the
native element are, of course, Roman Catholics.”’
He has often longed, he said, to see his sect ** taking
a more prominent part in the moulding of their national
life and character.” 1If his sect has not done that in
the past it was not certainly that it did not try. One
only need turn to last week’s LLEADER and read the
article on ** Episcopalians and Toleration ** to find that
out. Wé have seen that Arnott’s ‘‘ Times,’’ that has
not.even had as much as one line about the Oireachtas,
recently declared : “Itissad to think just now of all that
the League might have done for Ireland.”’ And the Pro-
testant Bishop of Tuam said : ‘‘It was sad to think how
little sympathy there has been in the past between the
National Church and the aspirations of she nation
itself.”” The cool assumption of styling the Church of
England’s Faithful Garrison in Ireland ‘‘ the National
Church!””  Now that Ireland is on the march the sec-
‘tion that for centuries attempted to kill it, the section
so well described as England’s Taithful Garrison in
_Il'ehmd are getting afraid, for what they dread most
is to be left outside. They are getting nervous and
jealous of a resurgent and masculine Ireland. The
Protestant Bishop of Tuam remarked: ‘‘For the
moment those who belong to the Church of Rome had
the ‘monopoly of all that concerned the welfare of the
people, and it was that Church that posed herself as
the guardian of their national life.” ~ Evidently the
progress of Ireland, despite all that England’s Faithful
Garrison have been able to do, is giving some of them
pause. We understand these gentlemen well. They
did their best against Ireland, and when Ireland
emerges triumphant they will tend to pose as friends,
and attempt to deflect and influence what they proved
powerless to destroy.

There is room for any class in this country when it
throws its lot in wholeheartedly with the country, but
we are not all so simple as to be deceived by the
spurious class of lip patriot, with his pretensions about
the ‘national church’ and his reference to the
““ Church of Rome,”” who would like to come in on his
own terms after having failed to destroy. We would
think just a little more of the sincerity of such gentle-
men as the Protestant Bishgp of Tuam if they
denounced the Orange atrocities, renounced Angli-
cisation and made open confession of the sins against
Ireland of the Church of England’s Faithful Garrison
in this country.

Did those perverted Britons see.
They saw a place of mental blight,
TFoul bigotry and racial spite,

An ignorance that never lightens,
And poverty that fairly frightens.
Oh, what a bed of loyal roses
And civic virtues that discloses.

- Last week we asked: “ But has Peamount purchased
peace by passing on its turmoil to the National Insur-
ance Commissioners >'? And sure enough Dr. McGrath,
who was duly elected to Peamount, but who apparently
waived his claim and took a job in connection with the
National Insurance Act, has wobbled and run away from
the job. We don’t think he will have any public sym-
pathy whatever now, and though the action of Lady
Aberdeen over the original election is rendered none the
less obnoxious by anything that has since happened,
many people will say that, though Dr. McGrath was
badly treated, he has since proved himself such a weak-
ling that he deserves very little sympathy. It was open
to him to fight for the position to which he was elected
at Peamount, and we do not see that the medical pro-
fession could have countenanced, at least without a
vigorous protest, any, other medical man taking up the
post if Dr. McGrath were defeated in the second elec-

* tion. Surely he had his wits about him when he agreed

to take the post under the National Insurance Act and
retreat from his claim to the Peamount position. Saw-
bones appears to have a very militant trade union, but
Dr. McGrath knew all about that when he agreed to
¥ce service under the National Insurance Act. If a
medical man, with all the facts before him, decides to
take a job, and takes it, and then runs away, he cannot
complain if he is laughed at.

CARSON’S PARLIAMENT.

In the course of a recent speech Carson said:—* I hope in September to call together the whole ‘ Ulster
Council,” and I hope we shall sit in our own Parliament from day to day, taking all the necessary means, by
Committee and otherwise, and by gaining information throughout the country through the clubs.”’

Szene.—The last refuge of—the British Constitution.
Carson, Craig, Londonderry, and Moore, with a
cat and dog, are in Parliament assembled. John
Redmond and Joe Devlin enjoy the scene from
outside. 3

CarsoN.—Most potent Craig and Londonderry,
Melodious Moore, the croaker merry,
We’re here to sit in grave debate,
Like pillars of a mighty state.

The British Parliament to-day
Is but a farce, a burlesque play
In which poor Carrion Crows distressed
Are made the theme of ribald jest.
7o such no more will we submit
But here as self-made rulers sit.
This box of rifles, sent to scare,
- Will be our legislative chair,
And severed from Imperial traitors
We’ll act grim tragic legislators.

Cratc.—Our grim and tragie zeal to show,

HERE are one or two other debating experience

- worth recording. There have been so many
imported loyalists in England during the last year or
two that it has been difficult to follow them up, and
the ordinary English Unionist speaker has had a very
poor show so far as Home Rule is concerned. I did,
however, meet one English Unionist orator in debate
on Home Rule at Hackney—Mr. T. J. Morrant, and
my encounter with him is interesting as indicating
the intellectual incapability of the ordinary Unionist.
In the course of the debate Mr. Morrant made a
number of statements about Ireland that caused me
(and the Home Rulers in the audience) to positively
gasp with astonishment. I requested his authority for
these fables, and was vreferred to ‘‘ Dowsett.”
Towders sawa this, and Dowsett said the other. Now
1 thought I kuew, at least by name, every author of
any importance on Irish questions, but 1 had never
heard of the learned Dowsett. I could therefore oply
‘quote acknowledged authorities against him, promising
myself the pleasure of an early investigation of
Dowsett’s credentials. Will it-be believed, Dowsett
turned out to be a religious crank of some kind who,
after publishing tracts at the time of the Bradlaugh
controversy, had written a book of the most ridiculous
t¢haracter about Ireland?- And this precious volume
was brought on the platform, and used as an authority,
by a professional Unionist orator. ~The debate, I
grieve to say, ended in a vein of hilarity, Mr. Morrant
and his friend Dowsett being too much for the risibili-
ties of the audience. Iour people voted against the
resolution in favour of Home Rule. %

last at Chew Magna, near Bristol. The Unionist
Associations of Ireland had—and for aught I know
have still—a body of men working in the West of
England with a centre at Bristol. Local Irishmen,
notably Mr. John Valentine, and Mr. David Cronin,
kept a careful eye on the operations of those gentry,

garian nobility. None of them spoke it and it is doubt-
ful if many of them knew it. Latin was the language: |

Hungarian is universal. It is spoken and written by |

instantaneous and dramatic was the effect .that in a
quarter of an hour the academy was founded. The
giver was then a goung nobleman, one of fashion’s
“favourites, supposed to be more concerned with sport
than with literature, yet his one act created a reform
and opened a new era for his country’s language. Latin
was then the only language spoken in the Hungarian
Diets. The young count took his seat there in 1826
wearing the uniform of an officer of Hussars. It is
impossible to describe the effect, however, produczd in
that assembly when he rose from his seat and ad-
dressed the astonished Parliament in their native lan-
guage. It was the first time that Hungarian was heard
spoken in that place, and the surprise of all and the
indignation of some were indescribable.  The whole
Court party, the West Britons of Hungary, were simply
furious, and the Count received a peremptory order to
rejoin his regiment in the country without a moment’s
delay. He replied by sending in his resignation, and
at the next meeting of the Diet he not only addressed
the astounded assembly, but appeared dressed in the
picturesque native costume of his beloved fatherland.
This further act of aggressive nationalism simply drove
the Austrian party mad with rage, but the great body
of the Hungarian people sympathised with the move-
ment. He touched their heart, he appealed to the
dormant soul of a nationality that only slumbered. The
fires of Hungarian patriotism only smouldered. ~The
Magnates might be indignant, the Bureaux alarmed,
the whole official crowd up in arms. It mattered little.
The seed was sown and like the mustard seed of the
Gospel it soon grew to be a mighty tree. Count Szec-
henyi went on with his revivalist work. He founded
the Casino of Pesth, a sort of conversational lounge for
young and old, modelled after the fashion of an English
club. He started races, jockey clubs, and various other
moans of social gatherings. In 1831 he published a
little pamphlet, Magyar Sinhaz, on the educational
functions of the stage, written in Hungarian, and in
1837, following up the lines laid down therein, but not
until after great opposition, the National Magyar
Theatre was founded and opened in Pesth. His appeal
to his countrymen to get rid of Austrian influences was
stirring and effective and might so well apply to our
own country. ‘‘ It is not Austria that oppresses you.
Tt is your own Gothic prejudices and mouldy institu-
tions.” No human power can arrest the life of a nation
if the nation be worthy to live. Your regeneration is
in your own hands.””  The effect of this publication
was immense. The Hungarian people soon showed
that their regeneration was in their own hands. The
nobles were accused practically of high treason to the
nation, and Count Joseph Dessewffy took up the cause.
He tried to answer the author of Credit in a work called
Analysis, but the Count answered in a book he desig-
nated The World, and the defender of the old regime
was discomfitted and soon retired from the field of con-
troversy discredited and defeated. The Government
took alarm at this dangerous resurgent patriotism, but
the great body of the Magyar nation roused {tSe}f to
take a deep interest in their great leader and his work.
The Count next published a work called T'he Stadium,
which was promptly censored out of existence, but it
soon found its way into the couniry from Bucharest.

The work contains the sketch of a system of laws which
are now the basis of Hungarian society. He was no
theorist but a practical man. He kept telling his coun-
trymen the splendid possibilities of their undeyeloped
country and what a grand asset they had in their noble
river if only the Danube were made navigable. It is
not;, possible, said the usual carping eritics who prevent
all progress. But it is, he said and showed. He built
a little yacht and himself navigated the river, and the
splendid bridge over it at Pesth, the tunnel of Buda,
and the explosion of the Tron Gates are undying records
of his activity. A journal was published in 1886 by
certain Magnates to report the proceedings in the Diet,
hitherto unpublished, and Louis Kossuth became its
editor, and from that position he rose by his genius to
be the leader of the movement of 1848. “Szechenyi was
always trying to bring about a better understanding
between the Austrian Government and the Magyar
people. He was no disruptionist, and his remarks are
singularly statesmanlike and wise. * Fairly to ap-
preciate the acts of the Government we must endeavour
to place ourselves at its point of view. We shall then
perceive that much which we attribute to Machiavelian
craft is only due to deplorable ignorance. Similarly it
is to be wished that the Government should be enabled
and induced to place itself more often at an Hungarian
point of view—that which is furnished by our constitu-
tional regime. Otherwise the most legitimate pre-oc-
cupation on behalf of our rights will be misconstrued as
seditious.”” Had that sound advice been taken how
much bad blood and :nisunderstanding that afterwards
existed would have been happily saved. He saw
that the destinies of his country lay in its development
of its splendid natural resources hitherto neglected.
In two years he achieved wonders. He found Magyar,
the native language, almost unknown. He made it a
living language in that time. He obliged the stern
Austrian Government to adopt it as the official medium
of intercourse with its Flungarian subjects. At his call
a national drama and a national literature sprung into
life. ‘“ When,”” says M. Saint René Taillandier, *‘ we
compare the moral and intellectual culture of the Hun-
garians previous to 1830 with what they have become
under the influence of Count Szechenyi the result seems
scarcely credible.””  “ Few men,” wrote M. Langs-
dorff in 1848, ‘‘ have effected more for the welfare of
their country than this illustrious citizen. The life of
Hungary for the last twenty years had its source in
him.”” Tt is to his genius that Hungary owes it that
it now occupies the commanding position it holds to-
day, not only in the Austrian Empire, but in FEurope.
Another lesson he taught which unfortunately his Hun-
garian countrymen did not learn, and that was the
necessity of sympathy and cordial co-operation with
their Slavic fellow subjects. They have yet to learn
that therein lies their true greatness and strength, and
that, as he foresaw, until Croatia gets its full rights
and the other States, the Hungarian or any part of the
great Austrian Empire will not be safe, secure, and
things between them satisfactory. But Count Szec-
henyi did great and glorious work and he resurrected
his country through the medium of its native language.
His name deserves to live beside the great men of his-
tory, and Hungary owes him a debt it never can repay.
